text: Recently I posted a robotic right colectomy and I got some very welcomed feedback about my approach and technique and I was really looking forward to recording another one and on my next one really focusing on more meticulously dissecting out the root of the ileocolic pedicle and even individually dissecting out the artery and vein and ligating those separately.
So this is a very high BMI patient, greater than forty, it's a male, and just a lot of intra-abdominal fat.
The surgeon observes the transverse colon encased in a thick layer of intra-abdominal fat, despite lifting the greater omentum above it.
The surgeon works to free atypical adhesions obscuring the transverse colon, gradually exposing it to restore normal anatomical visibility.

But after doing this for a while and evaluating his mesentery and anatomy for a medial to lateral approach, which is my typical approach, I felt that this gentleman would be a good candidate for a lateral to medial approach just to make it easier on myself and to have better visualization when I finally got to take the pedicle.

So I put together this quick video just to kind of illustrate pivoting based on the patient's anatomy and needing to feel comfortable approaching these cases in different ways. So I hope you enjoy and again As always, the feedback is very much welcome.

So after getting a feel of what it would be like to try to approach this medial to lateral through that very thick mesentery, this is where I changed the approach and decided to go lateral to medial for the majority of the case here.
I will kind of speed through this dissection.

reasonable person may ask well if this is an easier approach if this is the fallback well then why isn't this the standard approach and into that I would say the medial to lateral approach feels easier to me at this point for most patients most patients with more favorable anatomy with maybe thinner mesentery without so much scarring on that medial mesentery That does feel easier and quicker in the dissection.

But just with certain patients' body habitus, I think this lateral to medial approach is a good fallback. And I can feel a little bit more safe than just trying to directly dissect through that thick mesentery down to the iliocolic pedicle.
So I went from lateral to medial for quite a while here. Didn't quite reach all the way to the duodenum. You'll see that a bit later.

The surgeon frees the greater omentum from the transverse colon, defining the anatomy before mobilizing the hepatic flexure using a vessel sealer. The dissection planes from lateral to medial are joined.
The surgeon completes the hepatic flexure mobilization, connecting the two dissection planes. Further dissection is performed to reach the proximal transverse colon at the distal resection margin.
The surgeon identifies the duodenum and begins dissection on top of it, aiming to complete the lateral to medial dissection plane over the colon and viscera.

The surgeon places a Raytech sponge on top of the duodenum to aid in guiding the dissection through the thick ileocolic mesentery, which measures approximately two to two and a half inches in thickness.

The surgeon has reached the root of the ileocolic vessels, identifying the ileocolic vein through the ileal mesentery. The surgeon cleans the area and prepares to select the distal resection margin, proceeding through the colonic mesentery towards the root of the ileocolic vessels, leaving the ileocolic as the final structure to address.

So listen, I will be the first to admit that this is not the prettiest dissection. I went into this case or I guess I went into the idea of my next right colectomy that I would post to be a case where I took the criticisms of my previous post and really worked hard on more cleanly dissecting out the root of the ileocolic vessels, the right colic vessel, individually taking these after scissor dissection. But I found that this just was not the right case for that.

I'm continuing to learn, continuing to try to improve with every case. Heck, that's why I'm posting these things is so that I can learn from your criticisms and your ideas and especially from watching your surgeries as well. For this kind of patient with this thick of a mesentery, I felt that changing the approach was what was best for me and the patient. I felt that taking his ileocolic vessels with this white load was going to make me feel most secure. And ultimately, I am pretty satisfied, you know, with his outcome and with the result of the operation.

I do wonder, however, if I am in the minority of surgeons out there that would have felt like he or she had to change their approach to this case to feel more comfortable with it. So I look forward to hearing that back from any of you and also any hints that you have in this case particularly. about how to maybe position yourself for success with a medial to lateral approach, positioning his bowels.

Also about running into those strange cocoon-like adhesions around the transverse colon if you've seen that and how you dealt with that or what that might be from. And also about, you know, skeletonizing the ileocolic vessels through, you know, a two-inch thick mesentery. All these things, this won't be the last patient that I have with this type of body habitus. Heck, it probably won't be the last one in the next couple of weeks. So I really do look forward to learning from your experience and any hints that you might have for me for the next time I try to tackle this.
So thank you again for watching. I get a lot from your feedback and also from watching your videos. Take care.
